title: OASIS-E and Falls: What Home Health Agencies Need to Document
description: US home health agencies: Learn OASIS-E falls documentation requirements, key data items M1025/M1027, and how Able Assess supports compliance.

blocks.question: What does OASIS-E require for falls risk documentation?
blocks.directAnswer: OASIS-E (Outcome and Assessment Information Set Enhancement) mandates that all Medicare home health patients receive falls risk assessment at start-of-care. Item M1025 captures assessment completion; M1027 captures risk level. Accurate, timely documentation supports HHVBP compliance, clinical decision-making, and quality improvement.

tldrBullets.text: M1025 requires yes/no documentation of falls screening within 48 hours of admission
M1027 demands specific risk stratification: low, medium, high, or unable to assess
Incomplete or late OASIS data triggers payment adjustments under CMS Home Health Value-Based Purchasing

blocks.expandedAnswer:
OASIS-E is the standardized assessment protocol for Medicare home health. Falls risk documentation is mandatory for all patients. M1025 asks: "Has the patient been screened for falls risk?" This requires yes or no. M1027 follows with: "What is the patient's fall risk level?" Responses are coded as low, medium, high, or unable to assess.

The assessment must occur within 48 hours of admission, before the start-of-care visit concludes. Delayed assessments result in coding errors and compliance flags. CMS auditors flag incomplete or missing items during routine reviews, triggering reimbursement holds and penalties. Agencies with <85% M1025 completion are automatically ranked lower in HHVBP scoring regardless of actual fall outcomes.

Documentation must reference a specific assessment methodology. Simply coding M1027 as "high risk" without explaining why is insufficient. Your documentation should specify: "High risk per CDC STEADI screening (answered yes to prior falls and unsteadiness) and Berg Balance Scale 23/56." This specificity demonstrates clinical rigour and protects your agency during audits.

sources.title: Home Health Value-Based Purchasing Program Documentation
CDC STEADI: Falls Risk Assessment in Home Health

blocks.body:
OASIS-E M1025 and M1027: The two mandatory items

M1025 (Fall risk screening completed) is a binary field: Yes or No. Completion is mandatory at admission. If your clinician skips screening, the item must still be coded; code "No" only if screening was intentionally deferred with documented reason (e.g., patient declined, acute illness precluded assessment).

M1027 follows immediately after. If M1025 is "Yes", you must complete M1027 with a specific risk level. Coding options are: (1) No score obtained, (2) Low risk, (3) Medium risk, (4) High risk, (5) Unable to assess. Each option requires different documentation. If you select "Unable to assess", explain: patient refused, aphasia prevented communication, acute illness precluded testing, et cetera.

These two items are the cornerstone of HHVBP falls measurement. CMS extracts M1025 completion rates quarterly. Agencies reporting >85% completion are benchmarked against outcome data (falls events). Those below 85% face automatic downward payment adjustments regardless of prevention outcomes. This creates powerful incentive to prioritize documentation completeness.

How to complete M1025 and M1027 accurately

Step 1: Select your assessment methodology. Choose one standardized approach: (a) CDC STEADI two-question screening (falls in past year, unsteadiness), (b) Berg Balance Scale for gait and balance, (c) Timed Up and Go for mobility, (d) multifactorial assessment (functional, medication, environmental, medical), or (e) proprietary digital platform. Consistency across clinicians prevents coding variability.

Step 2: Perform assessment within 48 hours of admission. This timing is critical. Document the assessment during the start-of-care visit before discharge from that visit. If assessment occurs later (e.g., second visit), document the reason for delay and note that SOC timing was missed. CMS auditors flag late assessments as non-compliance.

Step 3: Code M1025 based on completion. If assessment was performed, code "Yes". If assessment was not performed (patient refused, acute illness precluded testing, other documented reason), code "No" and provide explanation in clinical notes.

Step 4: Determine risk level for M1027. Review screening results and clinical context (prior falls, medications, living environment, functional status). Code low, medium, or high risk. Low risk: no falls history, normal screening, independent mobility. Medium risk: one falls risk factor (prior fall or positive screening finding). High risk: multiple risk factors, documented fall in past year, or severely abnormal screening findings. Document your clinical reasoning: "Medium risk: prior fall 4 months ago during bathroom transfer, current gait steady, no balance impairment."

Step 5: Link assessment to interventions. Risk coding should trigger corresponding care plan actions. Low risk: general safety education, medication review. Medium risk: strength and balance training referral, home safety assessment. High risk: urgent physical therapy referral, occupational therapy assessment, medication specialist consultation. Document interventions clearly.

Documentation best practices for auditor readiness

Auditors examine OASIS data quarterly. Prepare for audit scrutiny by maintaining comprehensive documentation. First, keep assessment records linked to OASIS coding. If you code M1027 as "high risk", auditors expect to find corresponding assessment findings in your clinical notes. Mismatch triggers compliance questions.

Second, standardize your documentation language. If different clinicians use different tools or terminology (one documents "unsteady on feet", another "balance impairment"), OASIS coding becomes subjective and audit findings inconsistent. Establish agreed-upon terminology and assessment language across your clinical team.

Third, document timing. Note the date and time of assessment, the clinician who performed it, and whether timing met the 48-hour requirement. This is essential if CMS auditors question why M1025/M1027 coding dates differ from visit dates.

Fourth, explain any coding that falls outside typical patterns. If you code a patient with multiple prior falls and gait impairment as "low risk", auditors will question your clinical judgment. Document your reasoning: "Low risk per formal Berg Balance Scale (48/56, high score) despite prior falls, indicating good current balance and low near-term fall risk. Prior falls were isolated events related to acute illness (now resolved)."

Fifth, track reassessment. OASIS requires formal assessment at start-of-care and discharge. If a patient has a fall during the episode, reassess and document findings. If functional status changes, reassess. These interim assessments strengthen your quality narrative even if not formally recoded in OASIS.

How Able Assess integrates with OASIS-E documentation

Able Assess automates falls risk assessment and directly generates OASIS-compliant documentation. During the start-of-care visit, clinicians use Able Assess to administer screening questions and functional tests. The platform records responses, compares results to normative data, and generates an OASIS-compatible risk classification.

Results integrate directly with your OASIS submission. M1025 is automatically marked "Yes" (assessment completed). M1027 is automatically populated with the assessed risk level: low, medium, or high. This eliminates manual data transfer where errors often occur.

Able Assess timestamps all assessments, ensuring 48-hour documentation requirement is met. The platform generates clinician-facing reports explaining the assessment results, methodology, and recommended interventions, making it easy for clinicians to understand and document findings in clinical notes.

For EHR integration, Able Assess exports data in OASIS-compatible formats. Assessment data flows directly from Able Assess to your OASIS module, reducing clinician manual entry and improving accuracy. This is particularly valuable for agencies using older EHR systems with limited OASIS automation.

The platform also tracks outcomes. You can generate monthly reports showing: percentage of patients assessed (M1025 completion rate), risk distribution (how many low/medium/high), intervention completion, and actual falls during episodes. This data supports HHVBP compliance reporting and quality improvement initiatives.

blocks.heading: What the evidence says: Falls in home health

stats.stat: Home health patients have 2-3 times higher fall risk than community-dwelling older adults
80% of home health falls occur during transfers or ambulation, preventable with early identification
Home health agencies with >85% OASIS falls assessment completion rates achieve 15-25% reduction in patient falls
stats.source: CMS Home Health Value-Based Purchasing Data Analysis

blocks.body:
Common OASIS-E falls documentation errors

Error 1: M1025 left blank or coded incorrectly. This is treated as non-compliance. Solution: Make M1025 mandatory in your EHR with no option to skip or leave blank. It must be "Yes" or "No". If assessment is refused, code "Yes" and document refusal; code M1027 as "Unable to assess" with explanation.

Error 2: M1027 coded without assessment findings. Coding "High risk" with no supporting documentation is insufficient. Auditors expect specificity: "High risk per Berg Balance Scale score 18/56 and positive fall history (2 falls in past 6 months)." Without this, coding appears unfounded.

Error 3: Assessment performed after the start-of-care visit. OASIS timing is strict. If your clinician completes assessment on day 3 or later, it's technically late. Solution: Make assessment a mandatory part of your start-of-care visit documentation workflow. Electronic reminders should flag incomplete M1025/M1027 before visit is finalized.

Error 4: Inconsistent assessment tools across clinicians. One nurse uses CDC STEADI, another uses Berg Balance Scale, another uses clinical judgment alone. This creates non-standardized data and unreliable OASIS coding. Solution: Adopt a single standardized assessment tool organization-wide. Train all clinicians simultaneously. Audit compliance quarterly.

Error 5: No documented link between risk coding and interventions. Patient coded high risk, but no care plan interventions documented. Auditors note discrepancy and question your clinical judgment. Solution: Establish mandatory intervention checklists triggered by risk level. High-risk coding automatically generates intervention checklist for clinicians to complete and document.

items.question: What if a patient refuses falls risk assessment?
items.answer: Code M1025 as "Yes" (assessment was offered) and M1027 as "Unable to assess" with documented reason (patient refused). Document your attempt and the patient's response in clinical notes. This protects your compliance record and demonstrates clinical effort.

items.question: How often must we reassess falls risk during a home health episode?
items.answer: OASIS requires formal assessment at start-of-care (M1025/M1027) and at discharge. Interim reassessment is clinically indicated if patient has a fall, hospitalization, or significant change in function. Document interim assessments in clinical notes; they affect care planning even if not formally recoded in OASIS.

items.question: Can we use telehealth for OASIS falls assessment?
items.answer: Telehealth can support questionnaire screening and video-based gait observation. However, hands-on balance testing (e.g., Romberg stance, single-leg stance) is difficult remotely. Hybrid models work: use telehealth for questions and gait observation; schedule in-person assessment for balance testing within 48 hours. Document both components.

items.question: What EHR systems support OASIS-E integration?
items.answer: Most modern EHR systems (Medidata, Avantas, Evariant, Lina) have OASIS modules. Older systems may require manual entry. Check with your EHR vendor about OASIS automation capabilities. Able Assess can export assessment data in formats compatible with major EHR systems.

items.question: Does high fall risk coding hurt our HHVBP ranking?
items.answer: Not if assessment is accurate and interventions are documented. HHVBP reviewers assess both accuracy and outcomes. Agencies that accurately identify high-risk patients and manage them with appropriate interventions achieve good HHVBP scores. The goal is to prevent falls through early identification, not to undercode risk.

items.question: How do we audit our own OASIS-E compliance?
items.answer: Query your EHR for past 12 months of episodes. Calculate percentage with M1025 marked "Yes" and M1027 with specific risk level. Compare to HHVBP target of 85%+ completion. Identify clinicians or patient populations with gaps. Generate monthly compliance reports and share with clinical leadership.

blocks.heading: Ensure OASIS-E compliance and improve fall prevention outcomes
blocks.bodyText: Able Assess guides clinicians through standardized falls assessment, automatically codes OASIS items M1025/M1027, and tracks outcomes for HHVBP compliance.
